blocks.html:
If your app sends the same big block of text — a system prompt, a tool catalog, a shared document — over and over, the API can remember its processed form and skip redoing the work. That's prompt caching. Cached tokens are roughly an order of magnitude cheaper to read than to process fresh, making caching one of the largest cost levers available — often ahead of model choice and prompt trimming.

Everything about caching follows from one rule: it's an exact prefix match. The request renders in a fixed order — tools, then system prompt, then messages — and the cache key is those exact bytes up to a breakpoint. Change a single byte early, and everything after it re-processes at full price. This is why most caching failures aren't missing markers; they're unstable prefixes.

So design by stability: deterministic tool definitions first, a frozen system prompt next, stable history, and volatile per-request content (latest turn, injected state) after the last breakpoint.

The classic bug: someone helpfully interpolates "Current date: 2026-07-18 14:32:07" into the system prompt. Every request now has a unique prefix; the usage logs show cache writes accruing and zero cache reads — you're paying the write premium on every request and never collecting. Other silent invalidators: JSON serialized without sorted keys, tool lists built from unordered collections, per-session IDs early in the prompt, feature-flag-toggled prompt sections, and swapping the tool set (position zero — maximum blast radius) or model mid-conversation. Diagnosis is mechanical: byte-diff two consecutive rendered requests.

Operational facts: up to four breakpoints per request; default TTL five minutes (a one-hour option costs more to write); prefixes under a model-dependent minimum (~1–2K tokens) are silently not cached. The usage block reports writes and reads separately — verify, don't assume. Economics: writes cost slightly more than input, reads a small fraction; payback arrives at the second hit, and traffic arriving faster than the TTL keeps itself warm. Know when caching is the WRONG lever: context-window overflow (cost ≠ capacity), one-shot workloads (nothing repeats — pure write premium), and output-generation latency (cached input speeds prompt processing only). The lever-matching drill: repeated prefix → cache; deferrable volume → batch; over-tiered → smaller model; bloated unique content → trim. They compose.

items: Exact prefix match — one changed byte invalidates everything after it.
Order by stability: tools, frozen system prompt, history, then volatile content after the last breakpoint.
Timestamps in system prompts are the classic cache-killer.
Verify with usage fields: zero reads on repeated traffic = silent invalidator; byte-diff to find it.
Caching fixes repeated-prefix cost — never capacity, one-shots, or output latency.
